verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And the Angel of the Lord spake unto Philip, saying, arise and go toward the south unto the way that goeth down from Jerusalem unto Gaza, which is desert.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he arose and went.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It seems to me, this (Philip) was one of the seven; for from Jerusalem he would not have gone southwards, but to the north; but from Samaria it was “towards the south.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The same is desert:” so that there is no fear of an attack from the Jews.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he did not ask, Wherefore? but “arose and went.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And, behold,” it says, “a man of Ethiopia, an eunuch of great authority under Candace, queen of the Ethiopians, who had the charge of all her treasure, and had come to Jerusalem for to worship, was returning, and sitting in his chariot read Esaias the prophet.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: High encomiums for the man, that he, residing in Ethiopia and beset with so much business, and when there was no festival going on, and living in that superstitious city, came “to Jerusalem for to worship.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Great also is his studiousness, that even “sitting in his chariot he read.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And,” it says, “the Spirit said unto Philip, Go near, and join thyself to this chariot.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And Philip ran thither to him, and heard him reading the prophet Esaias, and said, Understandest thou what thou readest?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he said, How can I, except some man should guide me?” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe again his piety; that though he did not understand, he read, and then after reading, examines.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he desired Philip that he would come up and sit with him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The place of the Scripture which he read was this, He was led as a sheep to the slaughter; and like a lamb dumb before his shearer, so opened He not His mouth: in His humiliation His judgment was taken away: and who shall declare His generation? for His life is taken from the earth.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the eunuch answered Philip, and said, I pray thee, of whom speaketh the prophet this? of himself, or of some other man?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then Philip opened his mouth, and began at the same Scripture, and preached unto him Jesus.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how it is Providentially ordered.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: First he reads and does not understand; then he reads the very text in which was the Passion and the Resurrection and the Gift.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And as they went on their way, they came unto a certain water: and the eunuch said, See, here is water; what doth hinder me to be baptized?” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark the eager desire, mark the exact knowledge.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he commanded the chariot to stand still: and they went down both into the water, both Philip and the eunuch; and he baptized him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when they were come up out of the water, the Spirit of the Lord caught away Philip, that the eunuch saw him no more: and he went on his way rejoicing.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But why did the Spirit of the Lord bear him away?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Hereby) the occurrence was shown to be more wonderful.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Even then, the eunuch did not know him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Consequently this was done, that Philip might afterwards be a subject of wonder to him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For,” it says, “he went on his way rejoicing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But Philip was found at Azotus: and passing through he preached in all the cities, till he came to Cæsarea.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This (Philip, therefore) was one of the seven; for there in fact he is afterwards found at Cæsarea.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It was well and expedient therefore that the Spirit caught Philip away; else the eunuch would have desired to go with him, and Philip would have grieved him by declining to comply with his request, the time being not yet come. (a) But at the same time here was an encouraging assurance for them that they shall also prevail over the heathen: for indeed the high character (τὸ ἀξιόπιστον) of the (first) believers was enough to move them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If however the eunuch had stayed there, what fault could have been found?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: [But he knew him not]: for this is why it says, “he went on his way rejoicing:” so that had he known him, he would not have been (so) delighted.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And the Angel of the Lord,” etc. (Recapitulation, v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 26.) (b) See Angels assisting the preaching, and not themselves preaching, but calling these (to the work).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But the wonderful nature of the occurrence is shown also by this: that what of old was rare, and hardly done, here takes place with ease, and see with what frequency! (c) “An eunuch,” it says, “a man of great authority, under Candace, queen of the Ethiopians.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For there women bore rule of old, and this was the law among them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Philip did not yet know for whose sake he had come into the desert: (d) but what was there to hinder his learning all (these particulars) accurately, while in the chariot?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Was reading the prophet Esaias.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the road was desert, and there was no display in the matter.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe also at what time: in the most violent heat (of the day). (e) “And the Spirit said unto him.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not now the Angel but the Spirit urges him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why is this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then,” the vision took place, in grosser form, through the Angel, for this is for them that are more of the body, but the Spirit is for the more spiritual.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And how did He speak to him?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Of course, suggested it to him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why does not the Angel appear to the other, and bring him to Philip?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Because it is likely he would not have been persuaded, but rather terrified.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe the wisdom of Philip: he did not accuse him, not say, “I know these things exactly:” did not pay court to him, and say, “Blessed art thou that readest.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But mark his speech, how far it is from harshness alike and from adulation; the speech rather of a kind and friendly man.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Understandest thou what thou readest?” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it was needful that he should himself ask, himself have a longing desire.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He plainly intimates, that he knows that the other knew nothing: and says, “Understandest thou what thou readest?” at the same time he shows him that great was the treasure that lay therein.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It tells well also, that the eunuch looked not to the outward appearance (σχἥμα) (of the man), said not, “Who art thou?” did not chide, not give himself airs, not say that he did know.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: On the contrary, he confesses his ignorance: wherefore also he learns.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He shows his hurt to the physician: sees at a glance, that he both knows the matter, and is willing to teach.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Look how free he is from haughtiness; the outward appearance announced nothing splendid.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So desirous was he of learning, and gave heed to his words; and that saying, “He that seeketh, findeth,” (Matt. vii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 8.) was fulfilled in him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And,” it says, “he besought Philip, that he would come up and sit with him.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark the eagerness, the longing desire?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But should any say he ought to have waited for Philip (to speak), (the answer is), he does not know what is the matter: he could not in the least tell what the other was going to say to him, but supposed merely that he was about to receive some (lesson of) prophecy.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And moreover, this was more respectful, that he did not draw him into his chariot, but besought him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And Philip,” we have read, “ran to him, and heard him reading;” even the fact of his running, showed that he wished to say (something).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And the place,” it says, “of the Scripture which he read was this: As a sheep He was led to the slaughter.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And this circumstance, also, is a token of his elevated mind, (φιλοσοφίας) that he had in hand this prophet, who is more sublime than all others.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Philip does not relate matters to him just as it might happen, but quietly: nay, does not say anything until he is questioned.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Both in the former instance he prayed him, and so he does now, saying, “I pray thee of whom speaketh the prophet this?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That he should at all know either that the Prophets speak in different ways about different persons, or that they speak of themselves in another person—the question betokens a very thoughtful mind.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us be put to shame, both poor and rich, by this eunuch.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then, it says, “they came to a certain water, and he said, Lo, here is water.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again, of his own accord he requests, saying, “What doth hinder me to be baptized?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And see again his modesty: he does not say, Baptize me, neither does he hold his peace; but he utters somewhat midway betwixt strong desire and reverent fear, saying, “What doth hinder me?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you observe that he has the doctrines (of faith) perfect?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For indeed the Prophet had the whole, Incarnation, Passion, Resurrection, Ascension, Judgment to come.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And if he shows exceeding earnestness of desire, do not marvel.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Be ashamed, all ye as many as are unbaptized.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And,” it says, “he commanded the chariot to stand still.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He spoke, and gave the order at the same moment, before hearing (Philip’s answer).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when they were come up out of the water, the Spirit of the Lord caught away Philip;” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 39) in order that the occurrence might be shown to be of God; that he might not consider it to be merely man.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he went,” it says, “on his way rejoicing.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This hints, that he would have been grieved had he known: for the greatness of his joy, having had the Spirit also vouchsafed to him, he did not even see things present—“But Philip was found at Azotus.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Great was the gain to Philip also:—that which he heard concerning the Prophets, concerning Habakkuk, concerning Ezekiel, and the rest, he saw done in his own person.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Bel. & Dr. v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 36; Ez. iii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thence it appears that he went a long distance, seeing he “was found at Azotus.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (The Spirit) set him there, where he was thenceforth to preach: “And passing through, he preached in all the cities, until he came to Cæsarea.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And Saul, yet breathing out threatenings and slaughter against the disciples of the Lord, went unto the high priest, and desired of him letters to Damascus to the synagogues, that if he found any of this way, whether they were men or women, he might bring them bound unto Jerusalem.” (ch. ix.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He fitly mentions Paul’s zeal, and shows that in the very midst of his zeal he is drawn.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Yet breathing out threatenings and slaughter,” and not yet sated with the murder of Stephen, he was not yet glutted with the persecution of the Church, and the dispersion.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Lo, this was fulfilled which was spoken by Christ, that “they which kill you shall think they offer worship to God.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He then in this wise did it, not as the Jews: God forbid!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For that he did it through zeal, is manifest from his going abroad even to strange cities: whereas they would not have cared even for those in Jerusalem; they were for one thing only, to enjoy honor.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But why went he to Damascus?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It was a great city, a royal city: he was afraid lest that should be preoccupied.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe his strong desire and ardor (and), how strictly according to the Law he went to work: he goes not to the governor, but “to the priest.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That if he found any of this way:” for so the believers were called, probably because of their taking the direct way that leads to heaven.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And why did he not receive authority to have them punished there, but brings them to Jerusalem!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He did these things here with more authority.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And mark on what a peril he casts himself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He was not afraid lest he should take any harm, but (yet) he took others also with him, “that if,” it says, “he found any of this way, whether they were men or women”—Oh, the ruthlessness!—“he might bring them bound.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: By this journey of his, he wished to show them all (how he would act): so far were they from being earnest in this matter.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe him also casting (people) into prison before this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The others therefore did not prevail: but this man did prevail, by reason of his ardent mind.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And as he journeyed, he came near Damascus: and suddenly there shined round about him a light from heaven: and he fell to the earth, and heard a voice saying unto him, Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou Me?” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why not in Jerusalem? why not in Damascus?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That there might be no opening for different persons to relate the occurrence in different ways, but that he alone should be the authentic narrator (ἀξιόπιστος), he that went for this purpose.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In fact, he says this [both in his oration on the stairs], and when pleading before Agrippa.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Fell to the earth”: (ch. xxii, 6: xxvi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 12) for excess of light is wont to shock, because the eyes have their measure: it is said also that excess of sound makes people deaf and stunned (as in a fit) (ἀποπλἥγας).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But him it only blinded, and extinguished his passion by fear, so that he should hear what was spoken.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Saul, Saul,” saith He, “why persecutest thou me?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And He tells him nothing: does not say, Believe, nor anything whatever of the kind: but expostulates with him, all but saying, What wrong, great or small, hast thou suffered from Me, that thou doest these things?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he said, Who art Thou Lord?” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 5) thus in the first place confessing himself His servant.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And the Lord said, I am Jesus, whom thou persecutest:” think not thy warring is with men.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they which were with him heard the voice of Paul, but saw no person to whom he answered—for (the Lord) suffered them to be hearers of what was less important.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Had they heard the other Voice, they would not have believed; but perceiving Paul answering (some person), they marvelled.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But arise, and go into the city, and it shall be told thee what thou must do.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe, how He does not immediately add all, but first softens his mind.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In the same way He called the disciples also a second time.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “It shall be told thee,” etc.: He gives him good hopes, and (intimates) that he shall recover his sight also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And the men which journeyed with him stood speechless, hearing a voice, but seeing no man.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And Saul arose from the earth; and when his eyes were opened, he saw no man: but they led him by the hand, and brought him into Damascus” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 7, 8):—the spoils of the devil (τὰ σκεύη αὐτοὕ), “his goods” (Matt. xiii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 29), as from some city, yea, some metropolis which has been taken.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the wonder of it is, the enemies and foes themselves brought him in, in the sight of all!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And for three days he neither did eat nor drink, being blinded.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What could equal this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: To compensate the discouragement in the matter of Stephen, here is encouragement, in the bringing in of Paul: though that sadness had its consolation in the fact of Stephen’s making such an end, yet it also received this further consolation: moreover, the bringing in of the villages of the Samaritans afforded very great comfort.—But why did this take place not at the very first, but after these things?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That it might be shown that Christ was indeed risen.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This furious assailant of Christ, the man who would not believe in His death and resurrection, the persecutor of His disciples, how should this man have become a believer, had not the power of His resurrection been great indeed?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Be it so, that the other Apostles favored (His pretensions): what say you to this man?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why then not immediately after His resurrection?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That his hostility might be more clearly shown as open war.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The man who is so frantic as even to shed blood and cast men into prisons, all at once believes!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It was not enough that he had never been in Christ’s company: the believers must be warred upon by him with vehement hostility: he left to none the possibility of going beyond him in fury: none of them all could be so violent.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But when he was blinded, then he saw the proofs of His sovereignty and loving kindness: then he answers, “Lord, what wilt Thou have me to do?” that none may say that he played the hypocrite, he that was even eager for blood, and went to the priests, and flung himself upon such dangers, in persecuting and bringing to punishment even them that were in foreign parts—under these circumstances he now acknowledges His sovereignty.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And why was he shone upon by that light not within the city, but before it?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The many would not have believed, since even there (at Jerusalem when the people heard the voice which came from above, they said that “it thundered” (John xii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 29, supra, note 2, p.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 123); but this man was authority enough in reporting what was his own affair.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And bound he was brought in, though not with bonds upon him: and they drew him, who had expected to draw the others.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he eat not, neither drank:” he condemned himself for the past, he confessed, prayed, besought God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But should any say, This was the effect of compulsion: (we answer) The same thing happened to Elymas: then how came it that he was not changed? (ch. xiii. de Laud.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Pauli Hom. iv. §1, t. ii. p.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What (evidence) could be more compulsory than the earthquake at the Resurrection, the report of the soldiers, the other miracles, the seeing Himself risen?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But these things do not compel (belief) they are calculated to teach (it) (οὐκ ἀναγκαστικὰ ἀλλὰ διδακτικά).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why did not the Jews believe when they were told of these things?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That he spoke truth was manifest: for he would not have been changed, had this not happened; so that all were bound to believe.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He was not inferior to them that preached the Resurrection, and was more credible, by being all at once converted.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He had no intercourse with any of the believers; it was at Damascus that he was converted, or rather before he came to Damascus that this happened to him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I ask the Jew: Say, by what was Paul converted?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He saw so many signs, and was not converted: his teacher (Gamaliel, supra, p.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 87, note 1) was converted, and he remained unconverted.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Who convinced him—and not only convinced, but all at once inspired him with such ardent zeal?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wherefore was it, that he wished even to go into hell itself for Christ’s sake?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The truth of the facts is manifest.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But, as I said, for the present let us take shame to ourselves (when we think of) the eunuch, both in his baptism and his reading.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do ye mark how he was in a station of great authority, how he was in possession of wealth, and even on his journey allowed himself no rest?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What must he have been at home, in his leisure hours, this man who rested not even on his travels?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What must he have been at night?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Ye that are in stations of dignity, hear: imitate his freedom from pride, (de Lazaro, Conc. iii. §3, t. i. p.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 748. c) his piety.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Though about to return home, he did not say to himself: “I am going back to my country, there let me receive baptism;” those cold words which most men use!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No need had he of signs, no need of miracles: from the Prophet merely, he believed. (b) But why is it (so ordered) that he sees (Philip) not before he goes to Jerusalem, but after he has been there?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It was not meet that he should see the Apostles under persecution.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Because he was yet weak, the Prophet was not easy; (but yet the Prophet) catechized him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For even now, if any of you would apply himself to the study of the Prophets, he would need no miracles.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And, if you please, let us take in hand the prophecy itself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “He was led as a sheep to the slaughter; and like a lamb dumb before his shearer, so opened He not His mouth: in His humiliation His judgment was taken away: and who shall declare His generation? for His life is taken from the earth. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is likely he had heard that He was crucified, [and now he learns], that “His life is taken away from the earth,” and the rest that “He did no sin, nor deceit in His mouth:” that He prevailed to save others also: [and] who He is, Whose generation is unutterable.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is likely he had seen the riven rocks there (on the spot), and (had heard) how the veil was rent, and how there was darkness, and so forth: and all these things Philip mentioned, merely taking his text from the Prophet.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is a great thing, this reading of the Scriptures!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That was fulfilled which was spoken by Moses, “Sitting, lying down, rising up, and walking, remember the Lord thy God.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the roads, especially when they are lonely, give us opportunity for reflection, there being none to disturb us.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Both this man is on the road and Paul on the road: howbeit the latter no man draws, but Christ alone.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This was too great a work for the Apostles: and, greater still, in that, the Apostles being at Jerusalem, and no person of authority at Damascus, he nevertheless returned thence converted: yet those at Damascus knew that he did not come from Jerusalem converted, for he brought letters, that he might put the believers in bonds.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Like a consummate Physician, when the fever was at its height, Christ brought help to him: for it was needful that he should be quelled in the midst of his frenzy.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For then most of all would he be brought down, and condemn himself as one guilty of dreadful audacity. (a) For these things Paul deplores himself, saying, “Howbeit for this cause I obtained mercy, that in me first Jesus Christ might show all His long suffering.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Verily one has reason to admire this eunuch.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He did not see Christ, he saw no miracle: he beheld Jerusalem standing yet entire (συνεστὥτα): he believed Philip.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How came he to behave thus?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: His soul was earnest (μεμεριμνημένη).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yet the thief (on the cross) had seen miracles: the wise men had seen a star; but this man, nothing of the kind.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So great a thing is the careful reading of the Scriptures!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What of Paul then! did he not study the law?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But he, it seems to me, was specially reserved, for the purpose which I have already mentioned by anticipation, because Christ would fain draw to Himself the Jews by inducements from every quarter.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For had they been in their right mind, nothing was so likely to do them good as this; for this, more than miracles and all else, was calculated to attract them: as, on the other hand, nothing is so apt to prove a stumbling block to men of duller minds.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See then how, after the Apostle, we have God also doing miracles.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They accused the Apostles after these [miracles of theirs]; they cast them into prison: see thereupon God doing the miracles.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For instance, the bringing them out of prison, was His miracle: the bringing Philip, His miracle: the bringing Paul over, was His.—Observe in what way Paul is honored, in what way the eunuch.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There, Christ appears, probably because of his hardness, and because Ananias would not (else) have been persuaded.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Conversant with these wonders, let us show ourselves worthy.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But many in these times, even when they come to church, do not know what is read; whereas the eunuch, even in public (ἐπ᾽ ἀγορἅς) and riding in his chariot, applied himself to the reading of the Scriptures.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not so you: none takes the Bible in hand: nay, everything rather than the Bible.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Say, what are the Scriptures for?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For as much as in you lies, it is all undone.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What is the Church for?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Tie up the Bibles: perhaps the judgment would not be such, not such the punishment: if one were to bury them in dung, that he might not hear them, he would not so insult them as you do now.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For say, what is the insult there?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That the man has buried them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And what here?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That we do not hear them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Say, when is a person most insulted—when he is silent, and one makes no answer, or, when he does speak (and is unheeded)?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So that the insult is greater in the present case, when He does speak and thou wilt not hear: greater the contempt.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Speak not to us” (Is. xxx.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 10), we read, they said of old to the Prophets: but ye do worse, saying, Speak: we will not do.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For there they turned them away that they should not even speak, as feeling that from the voice itself they got some sort of awe and obligation; whereas you, in the excess of your contempt, do not even this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Believe me, if you stopped our mouths by putting your hands over them, the insult would not be so great as it is now.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For say, whether shows greater contempt, he that hears, even when hindering by this action, or, he that will not even hear?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Say—if we shall look at it as a case of an insult offered—suppose one person to check the party insulting him, and to stop his mouth, as being hurt by the insults, and another person to show no concern, but pretend not even to hear them: whether will show most contempt?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Would you not say the latter?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the former shows that he feels himself hit: the latter all but stops the mouth of God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Did ye shudder at what was said?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why, the mouth by which God speaks, is the mouth of God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Just as our mouth is the mouth of our soul, though the soul has no mouth, so the mouth of the Prophets is the mouth of God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hear, and shudder.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There, common (to the whole congregation) stands the deacon crying aloud, and saying, “Let us attend to the reading.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is the common voice of the whole Church, the voice which he utters, and yet none does attend.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: After him begins the Reader, “The Prophecy of Esaias,” and still none attends, although Prophecy has nothing of man in it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then after this, he says, “Thus saith the Lord,” and still none attends.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then after this punishments and vengeances, and still even then none attends.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But what is the common excuse?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “It is always the same things over again.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This it is most of all, that ruins you.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Suppose you knew the things, even so you certainly ought not to turn away: since in the theatres also, is it not always the same things acted over again, and still you take no disgust?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How dare you talk about “the same things,” you who know not so much as the names of the Prophets?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Are you not ashamed to say, that this is why you do not listen, because it is “the same things over again,” while you do not know the names of those who are read, and this, though always hearing the same things?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: You have yourself confessed that the same things are said.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Were I to say this as a reason for finding fault with you, you would need to have recourse to quite a different excuse, instead of this which is the very thing you find fault with.—Do not you exhort your son?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now if he should say, “Always the same things!” would not you count it an insult?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It would be time enough to talk of “the same things,” when we both knew the things, and exhibited them in our practice.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Or rather, even then, the reading of them would not be superfluous.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What equal to Timothy? tell me that: and yet to him says Paul, “Give attention to reading, to exhortation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it is not possible, I say not possible, ever to exhaust the mind of the Scriptures.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is a well which has no bottom.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “I said,” saith the Preacher, “I am become wise: and then it departed from me.”—(Eccles. vii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Shall I show you that the things are not “the same?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How many persons, do you suppose, have spoken upon the Gospels?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet all have spoken in a way which was new and fresh.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the more one dwells on them, the more insight does he get, the more does he behold the pure light.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Look, what a number of things I am going to speak of:—say, what is narrative? what is prophecy? what is parable? what is type? what is allegory? what is symbol? what are Gospels?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Answer me only to this one point, which is plain: why are they called Gospels, “good tidings?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet ye have often heard that good news ought to have nothing sad in it: yet this “good news” has abundance of sadness in it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Their fire,” it saith, “shall never be quenched: their worm shall not die:” (Mark ix.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Shall appoint his portion,” it saith, “with the hypocrites,” with them that are “cut asunder: then shall He say, I know you not: Depart from Me, ye that work iniquity.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Matt. xxiv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Surely, we do not deceive ourselves, when we imagine that we tell you in your own mother-tongue (῾Ελληνιστί) these good tidings?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: You look downcast; you are stunned; you are struck all of a heap, unable to hold up your heads.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Good news” should have nothing in it of a duty to be done, but rather should counsel what is good: whereas these “Gospels” have endless duties to be done.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And again, to mention other things, as for instance, Except a man hate father and mother, he is not worthy of Me” (Luke xiv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 26): and “I am not come to bring peace upon earth, but a sword” (Matt. x.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 34; Luke xii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 51): and “In the world ye shall have tribulation—(John xvi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 33.) excellent good tidings these, are they not!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For good news is such as this—“You shall have this and that good thing:” as in common life men say one to another, “What shall I have for my good news?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Your father is coming, or, your mother:” he does not say, “You must do this or that.”—Again, tell me, how do the Gospels differ from the Prophets?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why are not the Prophecies also called Gospels, good tidings?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For they tell the same things: for instance, “The lame shall leap as an hart.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “The Lord shall give the word to them that preach the Gospel” (Ps. lxviii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 11): and, “A new heaven and a new earth.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why are not those also called Gospels?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if, while you do not so much as know what “Gospels” mean, you so despise the reading of the Scriptures, what shall I say to you?—Let me speak of something else.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why four Gospels? why not, ten? why not twenty?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If “many have taken in hand to set forth a narrative” (Luke i.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 1), why not one person?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why they that were disciples (i.e. Apostles)? why they that were not disciples?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But why any Scriptures at all?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet, on the contrary, the Old Testament says, “I will give you a New Testament.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Where are they that say, “Always the same things?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If ye knew these, that, though a man should live thousands of years, they are not “the same things,” ye would not say this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Believe me, I will not tell you the answers to any of these questions; not in private, not in public: only, if any find them out, I will nod assent.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For this is the way we have made you good-for-nothing, by always telling you the things ready to your hands, and not refusing when we ought.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Look, you have questions enough: consider them, tell me the reasons.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why Gospels?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why not Prophecies?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why duties, to be done, in the Gospels?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If one is at a loss, let another seek the answer, and contribute each to the others from what he has: but now we will hold our peace.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if what has been spoken has done you no good, much less would it, should we add more.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We only pour water into a vessel full of holes.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the punishment too is all the greater for you.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore, we will hold our peace.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Which that we may not have to do, it rests with yourselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if we shall see your diligence, perhaps we will again speak, that both ye may be more approved, and we may rejoice over you, in all things giving glory to the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ: to Him be glory and dominion now and ever, and world without end.
